feedback.text: A major concern of digital fairness currently is that video game publishers make sure video games can never be played again some time after the customer purchases the game. Video games don't have an expiry date however, and many old games are as popular now as the day they came out. Making a video game unplayable is unfair in two ways: 1. Selling a game, then taking it back (to add insult to injury, the purchase price is not refunded) 2. When making a game unplayable, the publisher is taking away a completely unique experience, which for many players is priceless. For the above reasons, I ask that you consider destruction of video games as a subject in the Digital Fairness Act.
